content.text: Running LLM-powered systems at scale means token costs compound quickly. A pipeline that costs $0.01 per request looks harmless until you're handling 100,000 requests per day — that's $1,000 daily, or $365,000 per year. Cost optimization is therefore not a nice-to-have; it determines whether a product is economically viable.

LLM cost optimization is the practice of reducing the monetary cost per unit of useful work produced by an AI system — without sacrificing output quality beyond an acceptable threshold. It encompasses prompt engineering, model selection, caching, request batching, and architectural choices that collectively shrink the number of tokens billed, the price per token, and the number of redundant calls made.

LLM inference costs are proportional to token volume and model tier. At small scale this is invisible; at production scale it is the dominant variable cost. Unlike compute infrastructure, where reserved instances and auto-scaling give predictable economics, LLM costs can spike unpredictably when context windows grow or task complexity increases. Teams that do not instrument and optimize costs early often face sudden bill surprises that force hasty architecture changes under pressure.
Cost optimization also intersects with latency. Smaller, cached, or batched requests are typically faster, so the two goals often reinforce each other.
enables higher request volumes within the same budget.
make it easier to price features and forecast infrastructure spend.
— fewer tokens and cached results both reduce latency.
— fewer compute cycles for the same work means less energy consumed.
— cached results can serve requests even during provider outages.
— optimizations require instrumentation, testing, and maintenance. Some strategies pay back in weeks; others may not justify the complexity at low scale.
— routing simple tasks to cheaper models saves money but requires a quality gate to catch cases where the cheaper model underperforms.
— cached responses may be incorrect after underlying data changes. Every cache needs a sound invalidation policy.
— aggressive context trimming can remove information the model needs, silently degrading quality.
Cost in LLM APIs is computed as
(input tokens × input price) + (output tokens × output price)

. Optimization strategies therefore target: (a) reducing input token count, (b) reducing output token count, (c) using a cheaper model tier, (d) reusing prior results via caching, and (e) accessing discounted pricing via batch APIs.

A well-designed pipeline applies these levers in layers. A request first hits a cache (no LLM cost at all), then is routed to the cheapest model capable of handling its complexity, then sent with a trimmed prompt. The combination of all three is far more effective than any single strategy.
The diagram below shows the four main optimization levers and how they combine in a request pipeline:

attrs.alt: LLM cost optimization pipeline: request → cache lookup → model routing → trimmed prompt call, with four strategy pillars

content.text: Any production workload processing more than a few thousand requests per day.
When LLM spend appears in your infrastructure cost dashboard as a meaningful line item.
Before scaling a prototype: bake in cost guardrails before traffic grows.
When output quality is already proven and you want to find the cheapest model that maintains that quality.
When a subset of requests are repeated queries (search, FAQ, classification) — cache hit rates above 30% immediately justify caching infrastructure.

Before optimizing, measure. Log input tokens, output tokens, model used, and latency for every call. Build a simple dashboard showing cost per task type. This baseline reveals where money is actually spent.

Audit every system prompt for padding: marketing copy, redundant examples, and verbose instructions that a shorter version conveys equally well. Load context dynamically based on what the current task actually needs rather than injecting everything always.

Classify requests into simple vs. complex using a heuristic (word count, entity count, presence of code) or a tiny classifier model. Simple tasks go to a cheap model; complex or high-stakes tasks escalate to the flagship model. A cascade pattern tries cheap first and escalates only when confidence is low.

Exact-match caching works well for repeated identical queries. Semantic caching (embed the query, search a vector store for near-identical prior results) extends coverage to paraphrases. Set cache TTLs based on how often the underlying data changes, not a fixed number.
5. Use the batch API for async workloads

Providers like Anthropic offer a Batch API that processes requests asynchronously (within 24 hours) at 50% the cost of the real-time API. Any pipeline that can tolerate overnight processing — nightly reports, bulk classification, document indexing — should use it.

import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

# BAD: bloated system prompt (high token count)
bloated_system = """
You are an extremely helpful, friendly, and professional AI assistant.
You always provide thorough, well-reasoned, accurate, and detailed answers.
You are knowledgeable in many domains and enjoy helping users with their questions.
Please classify the following customer support message into one of these categories:
[category list...]
"""

# GOOD: minimal system prompt (low token count)
minimal_system = "Classify the support message. Categories: billing, technical, account, other. Reply with one word."

response = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-haiku-4-5",
    max_tokens=10,  # classification needs very few output tokens
    system=minimal_system,
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "My payment failed yesterday"}],
)
print(response.content[0].text)  # 'billing'
print(f"Input tokens: {response.usage.input_tokens}")  # dramatically lower

import anthropic
import json

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

def classify_complexity(query: str) -> str:
    """Returns 'simple' or 'complex'."""
    # Heuristic: short queries with no code or multi-step reasoning → simple
    if len(query.split()) < 15 and "```" not in query:
        return "simple"
    return "complex"

def routed_completion(query: str, system: str) -> str:
    complexity = classify_complexity(query)

    if complexity == "simple":
        model = "claude-haiku-4-5"    # ~15x cheaper than Opus
    else:
        model = "claude-sonnet-4-5"   # balanced quality/cost

    response = client.messages.create(
        model=model,
        max_tokens=512,
        system=system,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": query}],
    )
    return response.content[0].text

# Simple query routed to Haiku
answer = routed_completion("What is the capital of France?", "Answer concisely.")
print(answer)  # Paris — billed at Haiku rate

# Complex query routed to Sonnet
answer = routed_completion(
    "Explain the trade-offs between RAG and fine-tuning for domain adaptation, with examples.",
    "You are a machine learning expert.",
)
print(answer)  # detailed — billed at Sonnet rate

import anthropic
import numpy as np
from typing import Optional

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

# Simple in-memory semantic cache (use Redis + pgvector in production)
cache: list[dict] = []  # [{'embedding': np.array, 'result': str}]
SIMILARITY_THRESHOLD = 0.95

def embed(text: str) -> np.ndarray:
    """Placeholder — use a real embedding model in production."""
    # In practice: openai.embeddings.create() or a local sentence-transformer
    return np.random.rand(512)  # stub

def cosine_sim(a: np.ndarray, b: np.ndarray) -> float:
    return float(np.dot(a, b) / (np.linalg.norm(a) * np.linalg.norm(b)))

def cached_completion(query: str) -> str:
    query_vec = embed(query)

    # Check cache
    for entry in cache:
        if cosine_sim(query_vec, entry["embedding"]) >= SIMILARITY_THRESHOLD:
            print("[CACHE HIT] No LLM call made")
            return entry["result"]

    # Cache miss — call LLM
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5",
        max_tokens=256,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": query}],
    )
    result = response.content[0].text
    cache.append({"embedding": query_vec, "result": result})
    return result

A SaaS product that auto-categorizes customer support tickets started with every ticket sent to Claude Sonnet with a 2,000-token system prompt. Monthly LLM cost was $8,400 for 200,000 tickets. After optimization:
System prompt trimmed from 2,000 to 180 tokens (removed examples and boilerplate).
80% of tickets (short, single-topic) routed to Haiku; 20% complex/multi-part to Sonnet.
Exact-match cache for repeated ticket phrases ("password reset", "billing question") — 28% hit rate.
Non-urgent batches (overnight reporting) moved to Batch API.
Combined result: monthly cost dropped to $1,100 — an 87% reduction — with no measurable change in categorization accuracy as measured by a weekly human audit sample.
— guessing which model or call is expensive rather than instrumenting first wastes engineering time.
— removing context that turns out to be load-bearing silently degrades quality. Always run quality regression tests after trimming.
— caching without TTLs or invalidation hooks means serving outdated results indefinitely.
— routing every request to Haiku to minimize cost breaks user trust when quality suffers on complex tasks.
— instructions like "explain in detail" or "give a comprehensive answer" drive up output tokens unnecessarily.
— a bug in a retry loop or an unexpected traffic spike can produce a surprise bill before anyone notices.
Tag every LLM call with a feature/task label so cost dashboards break down by use case, not just by model.
explicitly — do not let the model choose its own output length for tasks where a short answer suffices.
Define a quality floor (e.g., human accuracy audit, automated evals) before downgrading models, and monitor it continuously.
Set spending alerts at 50%, 80%, and 100% of monthly budget so you can react before hitting hard limits.
Review cost per task type quarterly; usage patterns shift as features evolve.
Prefer structured outputs (JSON mode) for extraction tasks — they prevent the model from padding output with explanatory prose.

At high request volumes, cache infrastructure itself becomes a performance concern. An in-process dictionary cache is fastest but does not survive restarts and cannot be shared across replicas. Redis handles shared exact-match caches well. Semantic vector caches need an approximate nearest-neighbor index (pgvector, Pinecone, Qdrant) that can answer in under 5ms at p99 to not add meaningful latency to cached responses.

Model routing classifiers must be faster than the LLM calls they route. A heuristic (token count, keyword detection) adds microseconds; a dedicated ML classifier adds 20–100ms. Avoid using a large LLM to classify whether another large LLM is needed — the cost of the classifier would negate the savings.
— observability platforms that track token usage, cost, and latency per call with minimal instrumentation.
— 50% discount for async workloads with up to 24-hour turnaround.
— open-source libraries for semantic caching of LLM responses.
— unified API gateway with built-in model routing, fallback, and cost tracking.
— count tokens before sending requests to detect bloated prompts early.

Cost controls must not compromise correctness or security. A cache that stores sensitive user data (PII, proprietary documents) must apply the same access controls as the original data — cache entries should be namespaced per user or tenant. Semantic similarity caches carry a subtle risk: a query from user A might score highly similar to a query from user B and return user B's cached response, leaking information. Partition the cache by tenant or sensitivity level.

Budget caps enforced at the API gateway level prevent runaway spend from bugs. Set hard limits that stop traffic before they cause a catastrophic bill, not just alerting thresholds that page an engineer who may not respond for hours.
Instrument before optimizing — you cannot improve what you cannot measure.
Prompt trimming is the fastest first win: audit every system prompt for redundant tokens.
Model routing (cheap model for simple tasks) typically delivers the largest absolute savings.
Caching eliminates LLM cost entirely for repeated queries — even a 20% hit rate justifies the infrastructure.
The Batch API cuts cost 50% for any workload that can tolerate async processing.
Always pair cost optimization with a quality gate — savings that degrade user outcomes are not wins.
Set budget alerts and hard caps to prevent surprise bills from bugs or traffic spikes.
